author: @TimeSurfer206
text:
10-50 PSI? Almost sounds like one could fabricate something out of a standard water heater, if one had the skills and machinery to make the hatch. I'd really prefer stainless for this, though.
I'd make sure I had multiple pressure relief valves on them, though. One for release of pressure during normal operations, and 3-4 others at different levels: Top, bottom, and split the heights, something in there will hopefully bleed. I'd also have them feeding into a water bath, FAR from the heat source in case it's venting flammable gas. And WELL ventilated, as in, outside.
I'd also build a blast wall to hide behind.
And after a quick Google Shopping-Fu, plug-in autoclaves of 10~ish liters capacity can be had for like 80 bucks US. The one I looked at is preset for 120 C, and 15 PSI. Which should work.

replies.author: @humanistwriting5477
replies.text:
Viable. Net postive energy. Your creating an endothermic reaction, meaning the end result has stored some of that heat energy you put in there.
The U.S.S.R. and the United States used these types of reactions to produce fuel as a buffer for fossil resources durning WW2, although i believe initially they tried to keep the reaction rather dry, sometimes using hydrogen directly as an additive at that point in time. 
A simular, related process was also used to "gassify" wood *in situ, done on the moving vehicle* for use in automobiles by some of the axis powers during WW2.

And of course that's assuming that you have appropriately insulation and that your not wasting a bunch of heat, but given what's been done including self powered vehicular reactors in the 1930's id imagine you would have to work hard to get a negative energy conversion.

Try nickel as a catalyst, you get various bio crudes and if you use waste from paper production  (tall oil) you can get naphtha, a mix of petrols and diesel fuel hydrocarbons with the right acids.
I believe the paper I read on it used carbonic acid, literally just carbonated water.

author: @ghostwheelppk
text:
So, the pressure and temperatures are more than that of a cook stove pressure cooker. 

How about this: would the environment in a Diesel engine cylinder produce the proper conditions for HTC of an organic slurry?

In the same way that auto engines are converted to air compressors with modified cylinder heads, could an engine be modified to use some of its combustion chambers to process biomass into water and carbon solids?

It seems like carbon buildup is always the unwanted byproduct of internal combustion. Could careful control of the process make it the desired end product?

If one could control the amount of water and feedstock, you could also recycle the steam to add additional energy back into the system. 

An engine that squeezes the coal out of a septic tank. What a time to be alive!

author: @martinsaint9999
text:
Build a cheap incineration toilet! There is a huge market because the existing models are quite expensive (2.000 - 4.500 $) and it is interesting for off grid living and cheaper house building.

In one of her annual reports BASF wrote about a project of recycling plastics in this way but that was it. I never heard of it again.  We could collect plastic waste for conversion to gas the same way we collect paper or glass. In Germany the plastic waste is collected once a month in a yellow plastic bag ("Wertstoffsack") but as far as I am informed this material is not recycled as they say but instead burned.

replies.author: @justinw1765
replies.text:
CO2 is not what is primarily causing climate change though. Rather, the climate models only factor in the Sun's irradiance (visible light) as a warming source from the Sun, but in reality, the Sun can heat the earth in multiple ways, and the Sun was in a grand Solar maximum from about the 40's to 2000 or so, when it was pumping A LOT of extra energy into the earth (the irony being that Solar irradiance actually goes down during Sunspots, solar flares, CME's, etc). Though it has since calmed down, the oceans are a giant energy/heat reservoir and take a long time to dissipate/lose that accumulated energy. 

  Meanwhile, when the Beaufort Gyre finally releases all that pent up cold, fresh water back into circulation, it will majorly mess with the ocean circulation and plummet the earth back into an ice age--the weakneing/quieting sun (over a longer period of time--currently we are going back into a short/regular Solar max cycle) will not help in this regard either.  These are roughly 10, 000 to 13, 000 year cycles.  About 11, 000 years ago, the earth saw a temp change of around 10* C and then around 4* C world wide. 

Currently, there are major changes being seen in all planets of our Solar system minus Mercury--which we currently have a lack of good data on.  Human pollution and C02 are obviously not causing these major system changes in all the other planets. 

   With all that said, we DO need to address and clean up our environmental pollution--not because of climate change, but because of collective health reasons. 

   Btw, though I question mainstream climate science, I am not a "right winger" type at all.

author: @ericblenner-hassett3945
text: one point some may try, Vinegar as the acid.  The results will vary greatly as the Ph and chemical composition of various types of even the same brand and type will be different between batches.  The companies can get away with it as it just has to be ' food grade ' and not Chemical grade, which will have to be measured in percent molar solution or pure powders.  In the late 90s I was sent a Youtube link to a similar aparatus that bubbled the heated vapours through water and any excess waste gasses could be burned to heat the chamber and reduse the fuel used.  This is also the basis of Gasifyers ( spelling??) that has use running cars by burning waste wood products ( without the acid as they use wood types that are naturally acidic as the compost ).  As usual great video, good pictures to describe the process.  Next step would be checking local regulations on what constitutes a ' still ' or distillary regulations and research if you can make something thatbwould be practical to add to the rocket stove.

replies.author: @justinw1765

author: @walterrutherford8321
text:
Years ago in an organic chemistry class we put sawdust into test tubes and added heat from a Bunsen burner. Because the tube was relatively oxygen free the wood didn’t burn. But once the moisture boiled off it emitted volatile gases which flowed down a tube to a pipette where we burned them in a constant flame. The material left in the tube was mostly pure carbon, which of course is also burnable. I think this is similar to what happens in efficient wood burners like rocket mass heaters. Instead of unburned hydrocarbons going up the chimney they are burned.
I worked with a researcher who wrote a paper about turning plastics into fuel. Years later I saw someone else doing just that at an energy fair. He started by demonstrating dissolving LOTS of Styrofoam with acetone, ie fingernail polish remover. But for more complicated plastics he used a device, probably similar to this one, to create a type of diesel fuel. That was several years ago so I’m surprised I haven’t seen it commercialized.

replies.author: @justinw1765
replies.text:
In order to use Solar for this process, you really would have to optimize it. Paint it the blackest black that you can, use Solar concentrators though you wouldn't want to point a very pointed parabolic beam at it, but use a more diffuse/larger area "beam". Alternatively, using vacuum insulation around it would help a lot (and you could probably forgo larger, more involved solar concentrators). It is not too hard to make vacuum insulated, mostly glass panels, but it is somewhat expensive as you need high quality, strong and thick glass for this application. (Easier and cheaper to do, would be to use glass jars, and once sealed with the vacuum pulled, put it upside down over the tube). 

     Another option to use Solar, is to get a good size Solar panel, and then hook it up directly to resistive/heating type wire, and wrap the tube with it, and then put a bunch of insulation (rockwool would be the best) around it.

author: @idratherstayprivate
text: Awesome thing! I've looked into it a couple of months back in the context of reprocessing cat litter into carbon/fuel but found that none of the medical autoclave specs I came across could get up to the required pressure/temp for HTC to occur. Do you have any example products you can point at that do indeed get upto ~150 psi / 10 bar?

replies.author: @spex357
replies.text: If you search for pictures of autoclaves it is easy to pick the high pressure ones out as they look just like the one in the video, chunky stainless. China and India are two sources.

replies.author: @idratherstayprivate
replies.text: @spex357 Thanks. Yeah, I've seen these around but usually they are pretty small and super chunky (made for much higher pressures,) like the one Rob had in his video. I'm looking for something on the order of a few litres. Other than small experiments (which are valuable in their own right,) there's not much you can do with such a small apparatus.

replies.author: @ericlotze7724
replies.text:
I was looking into using either some sort of fancy biodegradable synthetic clumping litter (if i can even find one), or just wood pellets, and using an Anaerobic Digestion system for treatment of it.

*If* i could get the conditions right, I could maybe even use (wax) paper, heck even PLA bags too, but yeah.

replies.author: @spex357
replies.text:
@idratherstayprivate The high pressure ones are probably small for a weighty reason. As they get bigger the wall thickness becomes table flattening. 
Sadly I expect many would like a bigger one.

replies.author: @idratherstayprivate
replies.text: @ericlotze7724 We use wheat based litter that we buy here in Toronto. It can go straight into the municipal composting system, so it's pretty much carbonizeable biomass. If I could only find a decent sized container that can handle the pressure/temp, I'd have a cat's lifetime supply of HTC fodder :D

author: @vitordelima
text: Still it's very hard to convert the liquid part into something that mimicks typical fuels, trying to synthesize simpler fuels would be easier and more efficient. Or use it directly in a heat engine or turbine engine.

replies.author: @ericlotze7724
replies.text:
From what I’ve read, a similar technology, “Hydrothermal Liquification” makes a feedstock that is essentially a “drop in” replacement for Crude Oil in refineries.

The composition varies depending on feedstock, and I’ve mostly read papers utilizing Algae (it can (and sort of needs to be) wet, so this removes the costly drying phase of Algae Biomass production workflows), BUT

The only real “contaminants” are Salts and some metals (typically Iron, unlike all the nasties in most coal/some oils!).

A “De-Salter” (basically just water washes then separates the oil and salt water) can easily solve the salts, and Hydro-Demetalization is a developed technology.  Worst case scenario a new/underdeveloped catalyst needs to be developed.

Also as conventional reserves dry up, we move on to more and more “unconventional oils” etc which often contain more and more “nasties” (particularly Sulfur), so in a way Biocrude may require LESS pre-treatment than these crudes.

replies.author: @ericlotze7724
replies.text: I can’t speak much to the usage of Sewage Sludge/Refuse Derived Fuels/Waste Tires, but assuming proper sorting (no e-waste/lead etc!) i think the contamination would be the roughly the same small amount.

replies.author: @vitordelima
replies.text: @ericlotze7724 Good to know, but if fractional distillation could be skipped in the production of fuel it would be great, since oil is pumped already formed from the from the reservoir and those solutions need to cut as much costs as possible to be competitive with something so straightforward. The article "Preparation and Characterization of Bio-oil from Biomass" by Yufu Xu, Xianguo Hu, Wendong Li and Yinyan Shi has an overview of some methods.

replies.author: @vitordelima
replies.text: @ericlotze7724 Organic waste gasification produces hydrogen directly along with CO (both are very useful in the synthesis of many organic substances and hydrogen can be used directly).

replies.author: @ericlotze7724

replies.author: @lokiaverro4196
replies.text: doing it in water allows for 1: saving the time/energy of drying your feedstock, and 2: keeping all the organic bits in the liquid product: in the absence of water, they would volatilize and be lost as gases.

replies.author: @justinw1765
replies.text: I believe Robert has some videos on it. Essentially you can use a stainless steel tube with threads on both ends with some strong SS caps, from plumbing supplies.  You do have to be careful with the temps and times, because these homemade ones can't handle the pressure levels of the specifically built autoclaves. To be on the safe side, one could heat it within a matrix of sand or the like, so that if does explode, it would be less likely to cause damage. And if you're going to go that route, it might be more efficient to wrap it directly with resistive/heating wire (then apply a DC current), put some fiberglass or rockwool insulation around it, and then put it in the sand box.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: yeah it really is- have a quick look at google scholar you will see literally thousands of articles on this and they are all the same process - it's simple and fascinating

replies.author: @c3N3q
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering which brings me back to Why don't we have giant autoclave factories...

replies.author: @c3N3q
@justinw1765
replies.text:
@c3N3q   Because unless you are using renewable's, like Solar, it is somewhat energy intensive. Then also corruption may factor in some. I suspect that the wealthiest people of the world don't exactly want the average person to be more self sustainable and energy independent. 

     Also, making large enough autoclaves for mass production, is a challenge in and of itself. The larger the vessel, the thicker the walls will need to be. That's A LOT of steel. One could possibly get around this some by using a composites approach though. For example, you could take a Steel tube and then reinforce it with a combo of sodium silicate mixed with some potassium silicate and a little lithium silicate (all in a water solution) and S-glass fiberglass cloth.

replies.author: @forestMog
replies.text: @justinw1765 Or just wind coils of high strength fence wire on tubes with end caps bolted together with high strength bolts.

author: @blahdelablah
text: Wouldn't this add to the problems of climate change or am I missing something?

replies.author: @kevincrady2831
replies.text: If it's using biomass as feedstock, and using fuel it produces to generate the heat, then it should be carbon neutral, or even carbon negative if the carbon in the "charcoal briquets" is used as a soil amendment (terra preta) and thereby sequestered.  There would be the carbon footprint of the acid production process (to make the dilute acids used) to take into account, unless there are carbon-neutral or carbon-negative ways to produce those acids.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I suspect it would depend on how it was done and what use it was put to mate

replies.author: @blahdelablah
replies.text: @kevincrady2831 You could use it as a carbon sequestration technique, but burning the end products as fuel is not a responsible thing to do, especially as we need to reduce the levels of CO2 in the atmosphere.

replies.author: @justinw1765
replies.text:
If climate change was solely or primarily caused by C02, but it is not. The science on climate change is extremely cherry picked and myopic, especially when it comes to factoring the role of the Sun. (First clue, the Sun can warm the earth by other methods other than simple irradiance [i.e. visible light.] 2nd clue, the Sun was in a grand Solar maximum from about the late 40's to 2000 period or so, and pumping tremendous amount of energy i.e. heat, during this period, which of course the oceans have stored and take a long time to dissipate all that energy [decades]). 

   The irony being is that the Earth is actually heading towards another ice age. This will happen once the Beaufort Gyre releases all its pent up, cold fresh water back into the ocean circulation system.  Northern Europe will be hit particularly bad at first. The first winter after that happens will be brutal. Anyplace farther north that is kept more temperate by warm ocean currents will be immensely affected. 

  Historically, humans have always done the worst during colder periods and the best during shorter, but warmer periods. Not just in the context of longer term ice age cycles, but even in relatively recent times during periods like "little ice ages" i.e. Maunder minimums etc. It is not a "coincidence" that the human population has exploded during a relatively brief, but unusually warm cycle of the last couple hundred years or so. Main reason being farming is a lot easier during warmer cycles, and much harder during cooler and colder cycles.

replies.author: @blahdelablah
replies.text: @justinw1765 Two points for you to follow up on. 1. Go watch potholer54s video titled "Are we headed for a grand solar minimum?" and report back on what you find out. 2. The strengths of ocean currents are largely driven by the temperature differences between the equator and the poles. If the ice at the poles melts, there'll be less strong ocean currents, making it harder to distribute heat away from the equator. Do you dispute this?

author: @Buzzhumma
text: So an induction coil could be wound around this and placed in a thermo insulated box with a thermo temp switch

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: still need the pressure mate - so as long as it can cope with that then you are away

replies.author: @Buzzhumma
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering oh I meant on the hydrothermal auto clave . Just wind the induction coil around the bottom part. Do you think it would be a good way?

replies.author: @justinw1765
replies.text: @Buzzhumma    I would be more apt to wrap resistive/heating wire around it, then insulation, and hook it up to a Solar panel. Just easier than induction, though the induction would be more efficient. But who cares if you're using the Sun as an energy source to begin with. It is super easy too, since you don't need an inverter, batteries, etc. Literally just a Solar panel big/high wattage enough to get the heating you need. (Though you would probably want some kind of electrical regulator as to adjust temps).

replies.author: @Buzzhumma
replies.text: @justinw1765 yeah ok i see. That 250 degrees c should be easy enough with resistive wire and keeps it a far more reliable simple system . This is a perfect case of combined thinking to get a better outcome ! 👍🏻thanks mate

author: @stevetobias4890
text: Going back to my scuba diving days (about 25-30 years ago) your talking about 20 metres under water. That is a very achievable pressure from the most simple pressure cooker, even a simple home made pressure vessel. Then you mentioned coal or brickettes. This seems to be a simple home made experiment.

replies.author: @Skunkhunt_42
replies.text: Would need to modify/change the pressure relief valve on home pressure cooker. I wouldn't suggest the avg home tinker to try such in kitchen device. A vessel failure could do serious harm, even at 30psig. At 150psig hot acid vapor and shrapnel would be a bomb. The Boston marathon bomber used dry ice in a pressure pot with the psv blocked if I remember correct

replies.author: @stevetobias4890
replies.text: @Skunkhunt_42 very true but done right using the standard safety regulations, even common sense, would have you pretty safe

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it is mate - we have done it before - but like JM says care is needed and for someone not used to this sort of stuff rated equipment would probably be the best idea - or at least stick some sand bags around it and resist the urge to peer in !!

replies.author: @stevetobias4890
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering extremely well said mate

author: @cetate93
text: This would also let off a lot of Syngas while it's being cooked, right?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: well it is in a sealed pressure vessel - so only if the pressure exceeded the check valve limit

replies.author: @Skunkhunt_42
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering exceed the **pressure safety/relief valve** - sorta like a check valve but in industry at least, we can't swap check valves for PSV's. Psv is the only safe option, has alot to do with how they relieve pressure at 5-10% of set point as well as reseal once below

author: @angelusmendez5084
text:
That's amazing, thanks! 👏
Does it have to be with strong acids? I'm thinking about cheap production of lactic/citric acid through fermentation.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: no mate it has to be a mineral acid - sulphuric, nitric, phosphoric - which kind of makes sense as an organic acid would just hydrothermally carbonise - anyway it is a weak solution

replies.author: @dapain1576
replies.text: Nitric acid is the easiest to get a constant supply of at home. You essentially just need a plasma coil in a completely sealed glass container with an air intake from an aquarium bubbler. The outtake is just several tubes of water filtering the gas through which collects the nitric acid, The end of the line to let the air out is just let out into a line that goes outside the building. You're going to want proper ventilation in case anything is leaking, which is always a concern as the gas itself is toxic. Processing can take a good week or two depending on your water containers. You can check concentration using pH test strips. The first few containers should be high concentration which you can water down as needed. The system needs to have a good supply of renewable power for itself to keep it going otherwise your hydro bill is going to rack up. I would also do your research before actually attempting and not take my word for it.

replies.author: @elalcalde3362
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Thank you for all of your videos. You are an inspiration. I do wonder if you had an aerobic digester, could you use the sulphur acid from that in the hydrothermal carbonization process?
